title: Run a Stage 7 Science Fair
overview: Bring together everything from Stage 7 Science to run a science fair that teaches visitors the most important ideas from this stage through real investigations.

project.drivingQuestion: How can we use the science from Stage 7 to run an accurate, safe and exciting science fair for our school or community?
project.finalProduct: Create a science-fair plan with a live investigation, labelled displays, a safety checklist, a visitor survey with a data display, and a short explanation of your scientific decisions.

stages.title: 1. Foundations display
stages.prompt: Choose the two most important ideas from Units 1, 2, 3. Build a display that teaches each idea with a model or diagram, an example and a check question.
stages.evidence: Two labelled displays with models, examples and check questions

stages.title: 2. Live investigation
stages.prompt: Pick one investigation from Units 4, 5, 6 and run it live: state your hypothesis, follow the method, record results and explain your conclusion.
stages.evidence: A completed investigation with hypothesis, method, results and conclusion

stages.title: 3. Connections wall
stages.prompt: Create a connections wall that links ideas from Units 7, 8, 9 to everyday life, with at least three labelled connections.
stages.evidence: A connections wall with three labelled links to daily life

stages.title: 4. Present and explain
stages.prompt: Present your science fair. Explain at least three scientific choices, check that your conclusions match your evidence and identify one improvement you would make.
stages.evidence: Spoken, written or recorded scientific explanation

evidenceChecklist: A safety checklist for every activity
A connections wall with three links
A survey with organised data display

rubric.secure: Observations, measurements, models and conclusions are accurate and checked.
Hypotheses, fair testing, recording and conclusions follow the scientific method.
Labels, diagrams, tables or charts make the science visible.
Decisions are explained using appropriate science words and evidence.

questions.question: The smallest unit of a living thing that is still alive is called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: a cell A cell is the smallest unit that is still alive; organs and tissues are made OF cells.

questions.question: Which part controls what enters and leaves the cell?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: cell membrane The membrane is selectively permeable, choosing what passes in and out.

questions.question: In which state of matter are the particles held in a fixed, regular pattern and only able to vibrate?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: solid. In a solid, particles are locked in a regular pattern and can only vibrate on the spot.

questions.question: What happens to particles when a substance is heated?
options: they gain energy and move faster
questions.answer: they gain energy and move faster
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: they gain energy and move faster. Heating always gives particles more energy, increasing their movement.

questions.question: Gravity is best described as:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: a non-contact force of attraction Gravity acts across a gap, with no touching, and always pulls objects together.

questions.question: The unit of mass is:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 3 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: kilogram Mass is the quantity of matter, measured in kilograms.

questions.question: How many characteristics of life do all living organisms share?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: seven. All living things share the seven MRS GREN characteristics.

questions.question: Breaking down food inside cells to release energy is called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: respiration. Respiration releases energy from food inside every cell.

questions.question: Which of these is a property of a typical metal?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: conducts electricity - conducting electricity is a key metal property; dull, brittle and being a gas describe non-metals.

questions.question: Which property means 'can be drawn out into wires'?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: ductile - ductile means drawn into wires; malleable means hammered into shape.

questions.question: What is needed for a sound to be made?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: A vibration is always needed to make a sound; heat, light and magnets do not make sound by themselves.

questions.question: A guitar makes a sound mainly because:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: It is the vibrating strings that make the note, not the wood or the metal parts.

questions.question: A microorganism is best described as:
options: a living thing too small to see without a microscope;
questions.answer: a living thing too small to see without a microscope;
questions.hint: Use the Unit 7 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: A microorganism is a living thing too small to see clearly without a microscope.

questions.question: Which of these is a single-celled fungus?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 7 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Yeast is a single-celled fungus. A mushroom is a large fungus, not single-celled.

questions.question: Which of these is a physical change?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 8 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: melting butter Melting changes state only; no new substance is made, so it is physical.

questions.question: In a chemical reaction, the new substances made are called the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 8 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: products Products are the new substances made; reactants are what you start with.

questions.question: Electric current is the flow of:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 9 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: electrons Current is the flow of electrons around the circuit.

questions.question: Which of these must be present for current to flow in a circuit?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 9 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: a complete loop Electrons can only flow in an unbroken, complete circuit.
